So, how can we establish our trustworthiness? One way involves the slow but steady approach of being an always honest source of information, which eventually establishes you as a credible communicator in the eyes of those who know you. But what if you're dealing with people who don't have a history with you, who don't know you as a straight shooter? Is there anything you can do to gain instant trustworthiness from them? It turns out there is, but it requires employing a strategy that involves turning lemons into lemonade. Rather than yielding to the usual tendency to describe all of the most favorable features of any offer upfront, and reserving mention of any drawbacks until the end of the presentation, a communicator who references a weakness early on is seen as more honest. The advantage of this sequence is that with perceived honesty already in place, when the major strengths of your case are then advanced, your audience is more likely to believe them. After all, your truthfulness has now been established by your willingness to point at not just positive aspects, but at negative ones as well. For example, if on an online review site, a reviewer first confesses to having made past mistakes in his or her purchasing history, customers become more likely to buy products recommended by that reviewer. Now it's important to recognize what I am not suggesting here, that at the start of your presentation you say, before we begin, let me tell you all the things that are wrong with me, my organization, our products, our services, no luck. Rather, I am suggesting three things. First, if there is a drawback to be acknowledged, it should be presented relatively early in your message so that the credibility it provides will positively color the rest of your entire appeal. Second, between the drawback and your strengths, place the words but or however, which direct the tension away from the negative onto the positive side of your case. Finally, within your appeal, there is an ideal place for your strongest arguments, which you can use to undercut or overwhelm the downside. It is in the moment immediately following the admission of a shortcoming. When, bolstered by resulting credibility, the favorable features of your case are likely to be processed most deeply and accepted most fully.
